title: Here’s Why The AI Capex Bubble Is All But Guaranteed To Cause a Historic Secular Bear Market

US economy is also mired in a jobless
recovery, which would likely offset any
boost to wages from productivity boom.
So you could kind of scratch the wage
part of that off the equation just
because the labor demand with this
technology it's so different than all
the other technologies that come before.
You know, this technology is not
augmenting human labor, it's replacing
human labor. It's the first time we've
ever seen anything like this.
You know, it's so in my opinion I I just
think investors need to be aware that
this cycle is going to be unique, it's
going to be different, and we're already
starting to observe it in the data. So
this chart here shows true private
services payrolls we're already in
declining. We peaked in true private
services payrolls, which is private
payrolls minus health care, minus
education, minus social services, and
government. So basically anything where
the government is the employer the the
the consumer of last resort. We peaked
in that statistic in March of 2024, two
years ago.
Two years ago in an economy that was
growing at or above trend the entire
time.
And so you look at the green dotted
line, that's the trend line from
December 2009 to November of 2022, and
then the red line is the trend line from
November 2022 to present. Guess what
happened in November of 2022?
It starts with chat ends with GPT. The
dramatic underperformance of employment
growth relative to labor supply growth
supports our jobless recovery theme. So
if you look at
the median value of private nonfarm
payrolls relative to the quintile being
of the civilian labor force we're down
here.
Yeah, we should be growing the private
nonfarm payrolls at least with data
going back to 1948 at about 1%. But
right now we're dramatically
underperforming at about 0.4%.
So that's a problem. The dramatic
underperformance of employment growth
relative to corporate profits growth
supports our jobless recovery theme as
well. If you look at the So if you look
at the median value of private nonfarm
payrolls growth relative to the quintile
being of net corporate profits, which is
another leading indicator for jobs,
right now we're at 9.8 9.6%. You know,
so net corporate profits essentially
saying, "Hey,
the private nonfarm payrolls should be
growing somewhere around 2.1% on a
median basis with data going back to
1948. Again, we're only growing 0.4%
dramatic underperformance.
We have a dramatically underperformed
private nonfarm payrolls have
dramatically underperformed net retained
earnings as well. So net retained
earnings were at 27.6%
in the most recent value year-over-year
with data going back to 1948. We should
be growing private nonfarm payrolls
roughly around 2.5% on a median basis.
Again, we're at 0.4%.
And then finally, the dramatic
underperformance of employment growth
relative to equipment investment growth
supports our jobless recovery theme. So
if equipment investment, you know, right
now we're growing at about 9.6%. With
data going back to 1961, we should be
growing private nonfarm payrolls at
about 2.5%, which we're dramatically
underperforming that at 0.4%. Again,
this is not a labor supply thing, which
is the Fed which is what the Fed will
tell you and and and and the Wall Street
Journal will tell you. I just told you
that we're dramatically underperforming
in terms of labor supply as well. We're
at the fifth percentile reading of labor
supply and we're still dramatically
underperforming in in in in in labor
demand growth labor you know total
employment growth by about 60 basis
points there relative to the median
value. So this is not just a labor
supply story in my opinion. We think
this is a
economy and society are changing story.
And guess what? Economies and societies
change and as investors we have to deal
with it.
So let's keep moving. If you look at the
CapEx guidance from Amazon, Alphabet,
Meta, and Microsoft, which we got last
night,
it's calling for a doubling to 715
billion in 1 year.
I think was it
Ray Dalio or Stan Druckenmiller or Paul
Tudor Jones, one of them somebody quote
me on that in the community chat. One of
them said, "Hey, you know you're in a
bubble when something doubles in 3
years."
Well,
it's doubling in 1 year
>> [laughter]
>> in terms of their CapEx here. So they
see that the market's open for everyone
wanting to give them money, so they're
going to go take it, and that's exactly
what I would do if I was a CEO of one of
these companies, too. If you're going to
give me hundreds of billions of dollars
to invest to however I see fit, I'm
going to take it and roll the dice.
And so
wrapping up with this,
a secular bear market is likely to
follow the AI CapEx bubble akin to the
railroad, consumer durable goods, and IT
CapEx bubbles.
Why do we say that? Well,
it's a pattern.
So we saw
peak-to-trough decline of 54%
in the panic of 1857, and then followed
pretty quickly with another 35% decline
in the panic of 1873. We saw a
the Great Depression had a max drawdown
of 86%, and then the
dot-com bust had a max drawdown of 49%
for the S&P and 83% for the Nasdaq. I
think it was 89% for the Nasdaq 100. So
again, I'm a patterns, you know, my
brain works in patterns and dot
connecting and I see the dot connecting
here that hey, if we finish this AI
CapEx bubble and everyone's got one
position on and anything goes wrong, and
I tell you right now anything could go
wrong could be what's the societal
ramification of the true private
services payrolls having peaked 2 years
ago.
What does that look like in the polls?
Oh, does that mean we have more wealth
taxes on the national ballot? Does that
mean wealth taxes plus pro-jobs
legislation
goes to the national ballot
starts to do we see massive wealth
redistribution? Will we see
nullification if states like Florida and
Texas want to reject pro-jobs
legislation? Will we see succession if
states like California and New York are
so fed up with the dysfunction in DC and
DC's refusal to protect citizens and its
jobs and they just say, "Hey, you know
what? Stop paying federal taxes. We're
going to abide by our own thing here."
Again, this has happened before. This is
a country that went to a bloody a bloody
civil war
over people's economic prospects.
The Civil War wasn't about slavery at
the beginning.
It was about poor people expanding
westward and wanting the same economic
opportunity as the rich slave owners in
the south.
It wasn't about freeing slaves, it was
about poor people wanting economic
opportunity.
And I'm telling you right now, if AI
takes away millions of people's economic
opportunity, this is going to be a
guaranteed outcome from a perspective of
the regulation, the legislation, and the
backlash that we're going to see in the
political cycle. So I'll wrap it up
there.
Terry Sandal here ending on a very
cheery note there
